briefing.headline: A softer capital regime meets a hardening AI-governance clock: relief on the balance sheet, scrutiny on the operating model.
body: The prudential pendulum has swung toward relief. The cross-agency capital re-proposal tabled in March lowers requirements overall and formally rescinds the contested 2023 'endgame' framework; with the comment window now closed, drafting of the final rules has begun. Markets read the direction as constructive — spreads on financial paper held firm into quarter-end and the economics of traditional lending improved at the margin.

Where capital eases, conduct and resilience tighten. Supervisors have made operational resilience, third-party concentration and the governance of AI-assisted decisions the spine of the coming examination cycle, and the clock is now explicit: the EU AI Act's enforcement powers and general-purpose-AI duties take effect on 2 August. With roughly four in five firms reporting AI-related incidents yet only about one in five holding a mature governance model, the distance between deployment and control has become the supervisory story.

watching.title: The AI-governance gap
watching.note: ~75% plan to deploy agents within two years; ~21% have mature governance. That delta is the examination risk.

watching.title: Capital reform direction
watching.note: Lighter requirements may repatriate activity to the regulated balance sheet; watch the finalisation drafting.

watching.title: Concentration & resilience
watching.note: Correlated cloud-zone failures and the first CTPP designations push third-party risk onto the board agenda.

reg.title: AI Act — GPAI duties & enforcement begin
reg.detail: Commission enforcement powers and general-purpose-AI transparency duties take effect; high-risk obligations bite, with fines up to 7% of global turnover.

reg.title: Capital framework re-proposal — finalisation phase
reg.detail: With comments closed on 18 June, agencies begin drafting final rules that lower requirements versus the rescinded 2023 framework.

reg.title: DORA — Critical Third-Party Provider designations
reg.detail: Supervisors progress the first CTPP designations off the register of information; concentration-risk oversight enters its binding phase.

reg.title: CPMI–IOSCO — margin transparency consultation closes
reg.detail: Final window to respond on initial-margin transparency and procyclicality in centrally cleared markets.

reg.title: MiCA — transitional period expires
reg.detail: Grandfathering for crypto-asset service providers lapses in several member states; full authorisation becomes mandatory.

reg.title: Tokenisation — FCA/BoE call for input closes
reg.detail: Responses due on the regulatory treatment of tokenised funds and on-chain settlement.

reg.title: Operational-incident & third-party reporting — build phase
reg.detail: Firms scope the new serious-incident and material-arrangement regime ahead of its 18 March 2027 go-live.

controls.title: Recovery failover did not trigger in a trading outage
controls.detail: An hours-long disruption to trading infrastructure exposed a disaster-recovery failover that did not invoke as designed; recovery was manual and slow.

controls.title: Correlated cloud-zone failure overwhelmed assumed redundancy
controls.detail: A multi-zone provider outage defeated a redundancy model that presumed zone independence — a direct parallel to DORA concentration risk.

controls.title: Agentic AI deployed ahead of an accountability framework
controls.detail: Autonomous agents chained actions across systems with no defined escalation threshold and no cross-agent audit trail.

controls.title: AML programme implemented in name only
controls.detail: A sustained gap between a documented programme and its actual operation drew a record penalty under an 'implementation, not design' enforcement lens.

controls.title: Privileged-access recertification lapsed
controls.detail: Quarterly attestation for elevated accounts slipped across several business units; standing access went unreviewed.

controls.title: Trade-surveillance model drift
controls.detail: Alert thresholds calibrated to stale volatility produced a measurable false-negative gap.

board.headline: The week capital got cheaper and accountability got more expensive.
body: Two forces are pulling in opposite directions for the board. On capital, the proposed framework lowers the prudential floor — a genuine easing after a decade of accumulation. But the relief is conditional: supervisors expect freed headroom to be matched by demonstrable operational strength, not distributed as though the risk had simply disappeared.

On AI, the accountability question has sharpened to a point. With agentic systems now taking and chaining decisions, and enforcement only weeks away, the board's exposure is no longer the model that scores but the agent that acts. The institutions absorbing this fastest are reclassifying agent configuration as a risk control and naming an owner before deployment — not after an incident.
decisions: Confirm a named accountable owner and explicit escalation thresholds for every agentic-AI deployment ahead of 2 August.
Require a tested exit and cross-zone failover for the most concentrated critical third-party dependency.
Resolve how freed capital headroom is governed — reinvested in resilience or returned — with the rationale minuted.
board.statNote: of firms hold a mature governance model for agentic AI, against roughly 75% planning to deploy it.

essay.title: Our frameworks were built for models that score. We now supervise agents that act.
essay.lead: For a decade the central object of model risk was the score. A system produced a number — a probability of default, a fraud likelihood, a suitability flag — and a human decided what to do with it. Governance grew up around that division of labour: validate the model, document the assumptions, review the output. The artefact was a recommendation, and a person owned the act.
bodyTop: That division is dissolving. Agentic systems do not stop at the score; they take the action, and then hand it to another agent that takes the next one. The chain can cross systems, vendors and decisions with no human at any single link — and no oversight function holding a complete view of the whole. The governance built for recommendations has very little to say about a sequence of autonomous acts.
essay.pullquote: The risk is no longer the model that scores. It is the agent that acts — and the second agent it hands the action to.
bodyBottom: This is why the supervisory centre of gravity is moving from model documentation to operating-model design. The questions that matter now are not 'is the model accurate?' but 'where does the agent escalate, to whom, and on what threshold?', 'can we reconstruct a decision across the chain?', and 'who is accountable before deployment, rather than after the incident?' Agent configuration — what a system may access, what it may action, when it must stop — is a risk control, not an IT parameter, and the institutions that treat it that way are the ones who will pass the coming examinations.

The enforcement clock makes this concrete: the window opens in August, and retrofitting governance under scrutiny is the most expensive way to arrive. The firms that move first will not merely be compliant — they will have given themselves genuine permission to scale, which is the entire point of building the controls before, rather than after, the agents are already loose in the estate.
